questions.question_text: Which type of listing agreement is most commonly used in Maryland?
questions.correct_answer: Exclusive right to sell
distractors: Open listing
Exclusive agency
Net listing
questions.explanation: The exclusive right to sell listing is the most commonly used listing agreement in Maryland. It guarantees the broker a commission regardless of who procures the buyer, providing the strongest protection for the listing broker.

questions.question_text: A Maryland listing agreement contains all required elements EXCEPT a termination date. What is the status of this agreement?
questions.correct_answer: It is unenforceable without a definite expiration
distractors: It automatically expires after 90 days
It is valid but can be cancelled anytime
It renews annually until either party objects
questions.explanation: Maryland requires all listing agreements to include a definite expiration date. A listing without one is unenforceable. Additionally, Maryland prohibits automatic renewal clauses in listing agreements.

questions.question_text: Which element is NOT required in a Maryland listing agreement?
questions.correct_answer: A guaranteed minimum sale price
distractors: Property description and listing price
Commission rate and expiration date
Signatures of the parties involved
questions.explanation: Maryland listing agreements must include property description, price, commission, expiration date, broker authority, and signatures. A guaranteed minimum sale price is not a required element of the listing agreement.

questions.question_text: A Maryland listing agreement includes a clause that automatically renews for another six months. Is this enforceable?
questions.correct_answer: No, automatic renewal clauses are prohibited
distractors: Yes, if both parties signed the original agreement
Yes, but only for periods under 90 days
Only if the broker provides 30-day written notice
questions.explanation: Maryland law prohibits automatic renewal clauses in listing agreements. Every listing must have a definite expiration date, and any continuation of the listing relationship requires a new agreement with the seller's explicit consent.

questions.question_text: A seller signs an exclusive agency listing. The seller finds a buyer without the agent's help and closes the sale. Does the seller owe the broker a commission?
questions.correct_answer: No, the seller is exempt under exclusive agency
distractors: Yes, commission is owed regardless of who finds the buyer
Only if the buyer attended an open house first
Yes, but at a reduced rate of 50%
questions.explanation: Under an exclusive agency listing, the broker earns a commission only if the broker or another cooperating broker procures the buyer. If the seller independently finds the buyer without the broker's involvement, no commission is owed. This differs from an exclusive right to sell listing.

questions.question_text: A Maryland seller does not want to disclose the condition of the roof on the property disclosure form. What option does the seller have?
questions.correct_answer: Choose the disclaimer option for the entire statement
distractors: Leave the roof section blank on the form
Have the agent complete that section instead
Refuse to provide any disclosure form at all
questions.explanation: Maryland's unique disclosure system allows sellers to choose between disclosing known conditions or disclaiming knowledge of conditions. The seller can select the disclaimer route, but this applies to the entire statement. Even with a disclaimer, latent defects must still be disclosed.

questions.question_text: What makes Maryland's property disclosure requirements unique compared to most other states?
questions.correct_answer: Sellers may disclaim knowledge instead of disclosing
distractors: Sellers are fully exempt from disclosure duties
Only commercial properties require disclosure
Agents complete the disclosure form for sellers
questions.explanation: Maryland's Residential Property Disclosure and Disclaimer Statement is unique because it gives sellers the choice to either disclose known property conditions or disclaim knowledge entirely. Most states require sellers to affirmatively disclose known defects without a disclaimer alternative.

questions.question_text: The Maryland Residential Property Disclosure and Disclaimer Statement applies to which type of property?
questions.correct_answer: Residential properties of 1 to 4 units
distractors: All residential and commercial properties
Single-family homes only
Properties valued above $500,000
questions.explanation: Maryland's Residential Property Disclosure and Disclaimer Statement requirement applies to residential properties containing 1 to 4 dwelling units. Commercial properties and larger residential buildings are not covered by this specific requirement.

questions.question_text: A Maryland seller selects the disclosure option and reports a past basement flooding issue that has since been repaired. The buyer later discovers the flooding recurred. What protection does the buyer have?
questions.correct_answer: The seller disclosed in good faith based on known facts
distractors: The buyer can void the contract automatically
The seller is liable for all future occurrences
The disclaimer option would have protected the seller
questions.explanation: When a seller chooses to disclose and provides known information in good faith, the seller has fulfilled their disclosure obligation. If the seller honestly reported the past flooding and the repair, they are not automatically liable for a recurrence they could not have anticipated.

questions.question_text: A buyer receives the Maryland property disclosure form and sees the seller chose the disclaimer option. What does this mean for the buyer?
questions.correct_answer: The seller is not representing knowledge of conditions
distractors: The property has no known defects at all
The buyer waives the right to an inspection
The seller guarantees the property is in good condition
questions.explanation: When a seller selects the disclaimer option in Maryland, they are declining to represent their knowledge of property conditions. This does not mean the property is defect-free. Buyers should still conduct thorough inspections, and sellers must still disclose any known latent defects.

questions.question_text: A Maryland seller chose the disclaimer route on the disclosure form. During a showing, the listing agent notices the basement smells strongly of mold behind finished walls. What must occur?
questions.correct_answer: The hidden mold must be disclosed as a latent defect
distractors: No disclosure is needed since the seller disclaimed
Only the buyer's inspector is responsible to find it
The agent should note it only if the buyer asks
questions.explanation: Latent defects that pose a direct threat to health or safety must be disclosed regardless of whether the seller chose the disclosure or disclaimer route. Hidden mold behind walls qualifies as a latent defect because it is not readily observable through reasonable inspection.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is the BEST example of a latent defect in Maryland?
questions.correct_answer: Cracked foundation hidden behind drywall
distractors: Peeling paint visible on the exterior
A broken window in the living room
Worn carpet throughout the hallway
questions.explanation: A latent defect is one that is not readily observable and cannot be discovered by a reasonable inspection. A cracked foundation hidden behind drywall meets this definition because it is concealed and poses a potential threat to structural safety. The other options are patent (visible) defects.

questions.question_text: A buyer's agent suspects a latent plumbing defect behind walls based on water stains on the ceiling. The seller chose the disclaimer option. Which characteristic makes this a latent defect?
questions.correct_answer: It is not discoverable by reasonable inspection
distractors: The seller chose to disclaim property conditions
The repair cost exceeds $5,000
It was caused by deferred maintenance
questions.explanation: A latent defect is defined by three characteristics: it is not readily observable, cannot be discovered by reasonable inspection, and poses a direct threat to health or safety. The key defining feature is that it cannot be found through a standard visual inspection, regardless of the seller's disclosure choice.

questions.question_text: Which condition must a defect meet to be classified as latent under Maryland law?
questions.correct_answer: It poses a direct threat to health or safety
distractors: It reduces the property value by over 10%
It was present before the seller purchased
It requires a licensed contractor to repair
questions.explanation: For a defect to be classified as latent in Maryland, it must not be readily observable, cannot be discovered by reasonable inspection, and must pose a direct threat to health or safety. All three elements must be present for the latent defect disclosure obligation to apply.

questions.question_text: A Maryland seller knows about faulty electrical wiring hidden inside the walls but chose the disclaimer route. Must the seller disclose the wiring issue?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, latent defects must be disclosed regardless
distractors: No, the disclaimer covers all property conditions
Only if the buyer requests an electrical inspection
Only if the agent discovers it independently
questions.explanation: Faulty hidden electrical wiring is a latent defect because it is not readily observable and poses a direct threat to safety. In Maryland, latent defects must be disclosed regardless of whether the seller chose the disclosure or disclaimer route on the property disclosure form.

questions.question_text: A licensed Maryland agent wants to purchase a property for personal investment. What disclosure is required?
questions.correct_answer: Written disclosure of license status to the seller
distractors: Verbal notice to the listing broker is sufficient
No disclosure if buying through another agent
Disclosure only if the property is commercial
questions.explanation: When a Maryland licensee buys or sells property for their own account, they must disclose their license status in writing. Failure to make this disclosure can result in disciplinary action by the Maryland Real Estate Commission.

questions.question_text: A Maryland listing agent completes the property disclosure form on behalf of the seller to save time. What risk does this create?
questions.correct_answer: Disciplinary action for completing the seller's form
distractors: No risk if the seller reviews and signs it
Only a risk if the information is inaccurate
The form becomes void but no penalty applies
questions.explanation: In Maryland, the agent should not complete the disclosure form for the seller. The disclosure or disclaimer is the seller's responsibility. An agent who fills out the form risks disciplinary action. The agent's role is to ensure the seller understands the obligation and that latent defects are disclosed.

questions.question_text: A Maryland agent has a personal financial interest in a property being shown to clients. What is required?
questions.correct_answer: Disclose the personal interest to all parties
distractors: Decline to show the property to any client
Notify MREC before proceeding with showings
Transfer the listing to another brokerage
questions.explanation: Maryland licensees must disclose any personal interest in a property to all parties involved. This conflict of interest disclosure ensures transparency and allows clients to make informed decisions. Failure to disclose can result in disciplinary action.

questions.question_text: A Maryland licensee sells their own home but does not mention their license status to the buyer. What consequence may follow?
questions.correct_answer: Disciplinary action by MREC for nondisclosure
distractors: No consequence unless the buyer was harmed
A fine only if the sale price was above market
The transaction is automatically voided
questions.explanation: Maryland licensees must disclose their license status in writing when buying or selling property for their own account. Failure to disclose is a violation that can result in disciplinary action by MREC, regardless of whether the buyer suffered actual harm.

questions.question_text: A Maryland agent ensures the seller completes the disclosure form but notices the seller omitted a known termite history. The agent is aware of the past infestation. What should the agent do?
questions.correct_answer: Advise the seller to disclose the termite history
distractors: Complete the missing section for the seller
File a complaint with MREC against the seller
Say nothing since it is the seller's form
questions.explanation: While the agent should not complete the disclosure form for the seller, the agent has a duty to ensure that known latent defects and material facts are disclosed. The agent should advise the seller to include the termite history, as past infestations can indicate hidden structural damage.
